text: Web Serial API gotcha in Nereidas (src/lib/serialPort.ts): calling reader.read() per-query and racing it with a setTimeout leaks pending reads. When the query times out you exit the loop but the already-issued read() is still pending on the stream — when bytes eventually arrive they resolve that orphaned promise and get silently dropped. Next query queues a new read() behind the previous orphan queue, so subsequent queries also time out. Symptom for users: permanent "Timeout leyendo puerto serial" even when the device is replying. Fix pattern (committed 2026-04-22, cbd37be): openSerialPort starts a single background pump that reads continuously and appends chunks to a shared buffer on the handle; readFrame becomes a waiter queue that resolves when predicate(buffer) matches. Timeouts just remove the waiter; bytes keep flowing. Also dropped the cosmetic queryInfo() broadcast probe from connect — its failure mode was what triggered the first leak.
subject: Web Serial API per-query reader.read pattern
object_value: leaks pending reads on timeout; use a single background pump + shared buffer + waiter queue instead

text: Systel Cuora Max 30 SM balanza does NOT speak the documented Systel RS-232 protocol (from soporte.systel-global.com). It uses a proprietary "Passer/Checkout" network variant meant for balanza-to-PC sync via Qendra. Frame format: PC→BAL [BalanzaID][Fx][Params][XOR checksum]; BAL→PC [BalanzaID][Fx][ASCII response]. Settings: 115200 8N1. Known Fx: 0x01=live weight (response: 16 bytes, 6 ASCII grams + 'e'/'i' stability byte + 6 ASCII aux + checksum), 0x17=heartbeat, 0x02=info (broadcast ID 0x00), 0x27=config. Default BalanzaID is 0x14 (20 dec), configurable in balanza menu at Configurar Equipo → Conectividad → 1 Identificación.

text: SourceForge download gotcha for non-browser clients: URLs like `https://sourceforge.net/projects/<slug>/files/.../<file>/download` and `https://downloads.sourceforge.net/project/<slug>/.../<file>` serve HTML landing/mirror-selector pages when hit with `curl` or `wget` without a browser session — returning ~50KB HTML instead of the binary. text: Lantronix CPR Manager identification gotcha: if you see an app titled "CPR Manager 4.3.0.3" with menu items File/Com Port/Device/Tools/Help and a tree showing "All Com Ports (N)" with ports marked "Inaccessible", that's the Lantronix Com Port Redirector. It maps a physical COM to a virtual COM over IP via a Lantronix device server (EDS, UDS, XPort series). The "Inaccessible" label just means no Lantronix server has been bound to that COM yet — it's the default empty state, unrelated to whether the physical device on that COM is working. Systel redistributes CPR Manager on their support portal because some Cuora Max ETH variants CAN be used through a Lantronix server, but for direct USB FT232R connection CPR Manager is irrelevant. Don't waste time on its "Inaccessible" display when debugging direct USB-to-scale issues.

text: Systel scale protocol correction: Cuora Max uses the Passer/Checkout protocol family (documented in https://soporte.systel-global.com/downloads/Instructivos/Passer/Anexo%20-%20Comunicacion%20Passer.pdf), NOT the standard Systel RS-232 protocol (which I referenced earlier). The standard RS-232 doc explicitly lists 10 supported models (Clipse, Croma, Bumer, Maya, Flaier, Flaier Plus, Komba, Nexa, Pilon, Vita, Urbe) and excludes "Passer en todas sus versiones". Passer/Checkout has 8 selectable protocol variants, Argentina default = Protocol 3 (SYSTEL ARGENTINA), Brazil default = Protocol 4 (SYSTEL BRASIL PASSER). Query bytes happen to overlap with the standard protocol (0x07 = BEL/peso demanda, 0x05 = ENQ/peso pedido) but the response framing differs. Nereidas src/lib/systelScale.ts should be extended with the Passer/Checkout protocol set if future Cuora integration resumes — reference the Rev 4 PDF above.
subject: systel-cuora-uses-passer-protocol

text: Systel Cuora scale RS-232 protocol spec (Rev 9, reference PDF at https://soporte.systel-global.com/downloads/Instructivos/Comunicacion-RS232/): 9600 baud, 8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit. Five protocol variants: (0) send 0x05, get STX + 6/7 ASCII weight digits + ETX + XOR, or 0x11 if unstable; (1) send 0x07 0x07, get 5/6 digits + 'e'/'i' stability flag + XOR; (2) send 0x07 single byte, same response as protocol 1; (3) Torrey compat — send 'P', get CR + decimal weight; (4) CAS compat — send 'W', get STX + 5 digits + CR. XOR checksum = XOR of all bytes except the checksum itself. Weight is transmitted at scale-display precision (no hardcoded decimals). Cuora Max 30 SM (30 kg max) typically uses protocol 1 or 2 and has 5 weight digits. Use the raw-bytes viewer to pick the right protocol for a specific unit.
subject: systel-cuora-rs232-protocol-spec

text: Credential handling protocol when user pastes secrets in chat: (1) Refuse to use the pasted secret. (2) Name the specific services exposed. (3) Direct user to rotate immediately in the provider dashboards. (4) Redirect to .env.local file (gitignored, readable by Read tool) as the secure exchange mechanism. (5) Do NOT save any part of the secret to memorymaster, Auto Dream, or any persistent store. (6) Proceed with dry-run work that does not require the keys (e.g., writing migrations or code that will be executed later). This protocol applies even when the user explicitly intends the keys to be short-lived — transcript persistence + hook ingestion + cross-session memory make chat-pasted secrets a durable leak.

text: Diagnosing "SNMP doesn't work on my devices" for a network monitoring tool: the problem is rarely "SNMP is broken" — it's almost always per-device ACL restriction. Audit pattern: iterate every unique device IP, run `docker run --rm --network host alpine sh -c 'apk add --no-cache net-snmp-tools && snmpget -v2c -c public -t 2 -r 0 $IP 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.5.0'` and bucket by response. In one real WISP-style deployment, 14 of 66 devices answered `public` community (mostly MikroTik switches + Ubiquiti LTU/EdgeRouter/EdgeSwitch), 52 were blocked (all airMAX PTP + Panel CPEs). The blocked devices typically restrict SNMP ACL to ONE known NMS IP (the existing Dude server) — queries from any other source get dropped. Docker backend containers show up with the docker host's LAN IP via default NAT, so they're not in the ACL. Fix is infrastructural (whitelist the new host IP on each device's SNMP allowed-from list, or broaden to the /24), not code. Ping probe works fine regardless — use ping state for up/down display even when SNMP is blocked.

text: Misleading symptom pattern when OpenClaw is under backlog pressure: outbound HTTP from OpenClaw (Telegram `sendChatAction`, `sendMessage`, Discord gateway, internal ws handshakes from 127.0.0.1) all fail simultaneously with "Network request failed" / "gateway closed (1000)" / "handshake timeout", making the operator think Telegram or DNS is broken. But `curl https://api.telegram.org` from the same VM returns 302 in under a second — network and DNS are fine. The real cause is that the gateway process's event loop is saturated handling the backlog of dead connections from local restart-loopers, so its own outbound clients share-starve on the single Node event loop. Debug protocol: (1) always `curl api.telegram.org` OR whatever upstream is failing in the log, (2) check `ss -tln | grep <gateway_port>` for Send-Q near ceiling, (3) look for restart-looping systemd units in `systemctl --user list-units --state=activating` — they are almost always the upstream cause, not the gateway itself. "Telegram is broken" almost never means Telegram is broken.

text: RouterOS reboot integration pattern in dude-replacement app (commit 36dc791, backend/internal/modules/routeros): wraps github.com/go-routeros/routeros/v3 Dial with a 5s timeout goroutine because the library itself doesn't honour context cancellation on the handshake. The wrapper's Reboot() translates EOF-after-accept to nil success, because RouterOS closes the TCP socket immediately after accepting /system/reboot — a naïve caller would see that as an error. Auth failures get classified into a named sentinel ErrNotAuthorised so handlers can return 401 instead of generic 500. Endpoint POST /devices/{id}/reboot requires body {confirm:true} as the destructive-action gate; credentials default to admin+empty (the three lab MikroTiks on 10.10.100.120/130/140 accept this) but username+password can be passed per-request. Validation pattern: send with a known-wrong password to exercise the 401 path WITHOUT actually rebooting the box.

text: wezbridge dashboard bind behavior + CSRF interaction GOTCHA (2026-04-15): `server.listen(PORT)` with no host arg makes Node bind on `0.0.0.0` + `[::]` — all interfaces. Dashboard is automatically reachable from the LAN at `http://<machine-lan-ip>:4200/`. HOWEVER, the CSRF defense added in v2.4.0 (commit 3a9c5ca) only allows Origin `http://localhost:PORT` and `http://127.0.0.1:PORT`. Consequence: LAN GETs work (show the UI) but LAN POSTs get 403 (can't kill/spawn/handoff). To enable full LAN access, either (A) hardcode LAN IP in allowlist, (B) dynamically detect `os.networkInterfaces()` at boot and add them to allowlist, or (C) match only the port regardless of host for trusted local networks. Option B is the cleanest — survives DHCP rotation, no manual config.

text:
## Common Failure Modes

| Symptom | Likely Cause | Fix |
|---------|-------------|-----|
| Container exits immediately | Crash on startup, missing env var | `docker logs whatsappbot-final --tail 50` — check for missing config |
| Bot unresponsive but container running | Event loop blocked or memory leak | `docker restart whatsappbot-final` |
| "Module not found" error | Dependencies not installed after file copy | `docker exec whatsappbot-final npm install` |
| Dashboard blank page | React build not injected or stale | Rebuild locally, `docker cp dist/. whatsappbot-final:/app/dashboard/dist/` |
| "docker cp" forgot step | Deployed code but not dashboard | Always run the 3-step deploy (scp → docker cp → restart) |
| Permission denied on files | Wrong user/ownership in container | `docker exec whatsappbot-final chown -R node:node /app` |
| Port 3000 conflict | Another service on same port | Check `docker ps` for port bindings, stop conflicting container |
| WhatsApp session disconnected | QR auth expired or phone offline | Restart bot, re-scan QR code |
| MySQL connection refused | DB container not running or wrong host | Check `docker ps` for MySQL, verify `DB_HOST` env var |
| Messages not sending | Rate limited or invalid number format | Check bot logs for WhatsApp API errors |

## Browser HSTS Cache Clearing

**Chrome/Edge:**
1. Navigate to `chrome://net-internals/#hsts`
2. Under "Delete domain security policies"
3. Enter: `192.168.1.1`
4. Click "Delete"

**Firefox:**
1. Close all Firefox windows
2. Delete file: `SiteSecurityServiceState.txt` in profile folder
3. Or wait for HSTS max-age expiration

**Playwright (for testing):**
- Fresh browser context doesn't help (HSTS persists)
- Use `--disable-features=StrictOriginIsolation` flag
- Or use curl/wget for verification
